title: The Place for Pinot: Two vineyards, two terroirs, one Yarra Valley story
body_html:
Boat O'Craigo crafts two distinct single-vineyard Pinot Noirs, each drawn from our estate vineyards in the Yarra Valley: one nestled in a sheltered vale in Healesville at the foot of the Black Spur, the other from our higher-altitude site at Kangaroo Ground.

Each wine is thoughtfully crafted, with individual parcels of fruit kept separate by vineyard and clone throughout fermentation and maturation. This careful approach preserves the unique identity of each site, allowing it to shine through in the final blend and in every bottle.

Kangaroo Ground

Situated on the western edge of the Yarra Valley, Kangaroo Ground is a warmer site, characterised by rich black volcanic basalt soils and north-east facing slopes that maximise sun exposure throughout the growing season.

Elevated approximately 100 metres above our Healesville vineyard, Kangaroo Ground sits at the southern end of the valley and offers a truly distinctive terroir. The vineyard is planted on ancient volcanic soils — black basalt over clay — the remnants of long-extinct volcanic activity, which retain warmth and contribute to the depth and richness of the fruit.

Planted in 2000 and spanning 17 acres under vine, the vineyard is now well established, with vines that have matured into balance. This unique combination of aspect, elevation, and soil profile makes it the ideal home for our Black Cameron Shiraz, Braveheart Cabernet Sauvignon, and Kincardine Grenache, alongside 3.17 acres of Pinot Noir planted to MV6 and D4V2 clones.

The warmer conditions at Kangaroo Ground allow for a longer ripening period compared to our Healesville site, enabling the development of generous, full-bodied fruit with depth and structure. Careful vineyard management — including maximising airflow, utilising the heat-retentive properties of the soil, side-throw mowing to enhance under-vine warmth, and precise irrigation and timing of practices — ensures the vines remain in balance and consistently produce premium-quality fruit.

Healesville

Nestled in the foothills of the Black Spur Range, our Healesville vineyard is a cooler, more sheltered site producing refined, cool-climate wines. Set in the upper reaches of the Watts River, the vineyard sits elevated above the valley floor with a southerly aspect and is bordered by Graceburn Creek, creating a naturally moderated microclimate.

The soils are alluvial, layered with ancient river gravel and bedrock reefs, contributing to elegance, minerality, and purity of fruit expression. These conditions are ideally suited to early budburst and early ripening varieties, making Healesville the perfect home for Gewürztraminer, Grüner Veltliner, Pinot Grigio, Sauvignon Blanc, and Chardonnay, alongside our only red varietal on site — Pinot Noir — with 9.4 acres planted to MV6, G5V15, and D4V2 clones.

Purchased in 2003, with vines originally planted between 1998 and 1999, the vineyard now encompasses over 20 acres under vine. Many of the older vines are dry-grown, encouraging deep root systems and enhancing site expression.

Cooler than our Kangaroo Ground vineyard and receiving more than double the rainfall, Healesville offers a markedly different growing environment. Since acquisition, the vineyard has undergone continual refinement, including the careful selection of varieties best suited to the site, the introduction of diverse clones to build complexity and flexibility in winemaking, and the ongoing evolution of viticultural practices.

This considered approach ensures the vineyard remains in balance and consistently delivers premium fruit defined by finesse, vibrancy, and a true sense of place.

See our Place for Pinot offer here. Book a table.

title: How Long Does Open Wine Last?
body_html:
Didn't quite finish the bottle? Here's our recommendations on how long wine will last after opening, and tips to make it last longer.

At some point, every wine drinker has held their nose over an open bottle of days-old wine and wondered, "is this still good?" But the answer to that question isn't a simple one. There are a number of factors at play: the type of wine, how it was stored, and even how much is left in the bottle.

While every wine is different, there are some general guidelines to follow that will help slow down the ageing process before it goes bad. The main chemical reaction that happens to open wine is oxidation. Once the wine's open you expose it to oxygen. It will start reacting just like, for example, if you left a piece of apple outside. The apple will turn brown after a certain time.

But isn't oxygen good for wine? Aren't we meant to let our wine breathe and swirl the glass to open up the flavours? Yes and no. A little bit of air is what's recommended to freshen up the wine. Oxidation, however, is when the wine has been overexposed and the oxygen starts to harm or overpower the beautiful aromatics and flavours of the wine.

How Long Will Open Wine Last?

According to experts, open white wine and rosé will last roughly three to five days if kept refrigerated. Sparkling wine is more like one to two days, but invest in a proper sparkling bottle stopper to save bubble loss. An open red wine, if closed immediately and kept in a cool dark place, lasts four to five days in winter but in summer it's more like two or three days. And finally, the last category to consider is natural or orange wine. Since a lot of these wines are lo-fi with minimal intervention they're best finished on the day they're opened.

How to Store Open Wine

To make your open bottle of wine last longer you have to fight off oxidation. For chilled wines like white, sparkling, or rosé, the key is to seal the bottles as soon as you can and pop them straight back in the fridge. That way there's temperature control, there's no light exposed to the bottle, and you're limiting the amount of oxygen that interacts with the wine. For red wines the same rule about sealing it ASAP still applies, however they don't necessarily need the fridge — a cool dark spot will be fine.

Tips to Make Open Wine Last Longer

Keeping a wine sealed is the most important step in fighting oxidation. Investing in a bottle stopper with a good seal will help keep air out of open bottles. Unfortunately the old trick of putting a teaspoon in the neck of an open bottle of sparkling will do nothing to preserve its lifespan. Instead, save those precious bubbles with a proper sparkling stopper.

A favourite hack is to transfer leftover wine into a smaller bottle to reduce the surface area exposed to air. A standard wine bottle is 750mL but some wines are available in 375mL half-bottles. Keeping an empty half-bottle on hand means you'll have the perfect vessel to transfer leftover wine into.

How to Tell When Your Wine Has Gone Bad (and when to pour it down the sink...)

The best way to find out if your wine has gone bad is by smelling it. You'll know when something's not right, the first sign is dullness and a lack of flavour. When you smell, you kind of get a musty fruit smell. Almost like if you cut a piece of fruit, left it out, and it started rotting.

Is there any way to save wine that's gone bad? Sadly no, there's really not.

title: In the Heart of the Volcano
attribution: Feature interview with vineyard manager Andrew Mills, written by Robyn Harper for Wineries of Victoria
body_html:
This feature originally appeared on Wineries of Victoria. Robyn Harper sat down with Boat O'Craigo's vineyard manager, Andrew Mills, to talk about the black volcanic soil at our Kangaroo Ground vineyard, and how it shapes our red wines.

Boat O'Craigo grows fruit from two distinct sites in the Yarra Valley: Healesville, with its cool, alluvial soils suited to white and Pinot varieties, and Kangaroo Ground, perched on an extinct volcano with a cap of black volcanic soil and dark brown clay over floating basalt subsoil. According to Mills, that black soil creates a warming effect that lets Boat O'Craigo ripen reds — Shiraz, Cabernet Sauvignon, Grenache, Gamay and two trial Pinot clones — even in difficult, wet seasons, producing intense flavour, colour and balance. Boat O'Craigo is the only Yarra Valley vineyard with black volcanic soil, positioned at the highest point of the extinct volcano.

The soil's roughly 300mm black cap is nutrient-rich and mulch-like: plastic when wet, crumbly and firm when dry, protecting the subsoil, preventing erosion and root compaction, and optimising drainage. Its warming, mineral-rich properties impart ripeness and help maintain balance and acidity through the growing season.

Boat O'Craigo maintains the soil with a primarily organic program — mushroom compost and other composts to encourage biodiversity, and side-thrown mid-row cover crops for both sun reflection and added organic material. The site's quality is reflected in a run of award-winning wines scoring in the high nineties, including the 2015 First Duke Shiraz, 2015 Braveheart Cabernet Sauvignon and 2015 Black Cameron Shiraz, which together contributed to Boat O'Craigo's James Halliday 'Dark Horse Award'.

Read the original feature at Wineries of Victoria.

title: Our New Vintage Release: 2023 Grüner Veltliner
body_html:
Austria's most planted variety, Grüner Veltliner, is slowly growing a healthy fan base in Australia, with the importation of clones from Austria by Adelaide Hills' Hahndorf Hill Winery in 2006–2009 kicking off its popularity.

Not familiar? Grüner Veltliner is a popular white grape variety, renowned for its crisp, refreshing characteristics. Versatile and capable of producing a range of wine styles, from light and zesty to complex and full-bodied.

Grüner Veltliner typically features flavours of green apple, white pepper and citrus, and is known for mineral notes and high linear acidity that echoes Riesling. Grüner's versatility makes it a fantastic match for seafood, salads, Asian cuisine, and spicy foods.

Boat O'Craigo is currently the only vineyard in the Yarra Valley growing Grüner Veltliner, and we are excited to release our new 2023 vintage, now available online and for tasting at the cellar door.

title: Deep Dive: Searching for the Best Pinot Noir in Yarra Valley
attribution: Tasting panel excerpt from Young Gun of Wine's Yarra Valley Pinot Noir deep dive
body_html:
Taken from our review from Young Gun of Wine, click through to read the full article.

Cabernet lovers may scoff, but Chardonnay and Pinot Noir are undoubtedly the hero varieties of the Yarra Valley, with Pinot leading the plantings by some margin. Young Gun of Wine gathered every Yarra Valley Pinot Noir it could find and asked an expert panel — Dr Ray Nadeson (Lethbridge Estate), Chris Ryan DipWSET (Trader House), Sarah Fagan (Tarrawarra Estate), Abbey Moret DipWSET (Atlas Vinifera), Patrick Dowling (Domaine Wine Shippers), Yuki Hirose MS (LUCAS Restaurants), Travis Howe (Carlton Wine Room) and Adrian Santolin (Santolin / Rob Dolan Wines) — to taste blind and name their top six.

Our 2022 Black Spur Pinot Noir ($40 RRP) made the top-six lists of both Hirose and Santolin. Hirose described "framboise and bright red cherry... carbonic nose with menthol-like cool characters, red roses, Turkish delight... very pretty in style, crunchy acid keeping the wine in shape." Santolin wrote of "loads of bright fruit on the nose — fragrant red cherry, raspberry, blood plum... a very elegant and delicate spice-infused palate, with cherry and rhubarb pushing through followed by some super-fine tannins."

Shop our 2022 Black Spur Pinot Noir.

title: The Best Dog-Friendly Wineries in the Yarra Valley
attribution: Excerpt from a Pooches and Pinot degustation tour write-up by Oliver, Winky & Iro of It's Woofs
body_html:
The dog blog It's Woofs joined a Pooches and Pinot Degustation Wine Tour through the Yarra Valley — a dog-friendly wine tour company run by Jeff and Helen — and stopped in at Boat O'Craigo along the way.

At Boat O'Craigo, the tour tasted five wines paired with five chocolates from the Cuvée Chocolates range, plus a brownie, and found the pairing (salted caramel especially) a pleasant surprise. Their dogs, Ollie and Winks, were welcome to run around on the lawn, and staff were happy to make a fuss of them — "the pawfect ending" to their day touring the Valley.

Read the full extended article at It's Woofs.

title: Orange Wine? No, It's Not Made From Oranges...
attribution: Article by Mitch Parker, originally published by Dan Murphy's
body_html:
This explainer on orange wine was originally written by Mitch Parker for Dan Murphy's.

Orange wine gets its name from its colour, not the fruit — it's made from white grapes given "skin contact," where the juice ferments with the skins and seeds much like a red wine, producing an orangey, amber hue. The longer the skin contact, the deeper the colour and sharper the flavour. It's closely related to rosé, which is the opposite approach: a red grape given only brief skin contact.

Sauvignon Blanc is a popular base for Australian orange wine because it stays crisp through fermentation on skins. The style is versatile at the table, pairing well with red meat, fish and vegetables alike, and works nicely as an aperitif. Serve it a little warmer than white wine and a little cooler than red — chilled, then out of the fridge for around 20 minutes before pouring.

Read the full original article at Dan Murphy's Dan's Daily.

title: We're Crushing Our Sustainability Targets
body_html:
At Boat O'Craigo we are proud to make sustainability one of our core aims, particularly in the hospitality industry where managing glass waste is often a messy, heavy and sometimes dangerous activity. We are proud to partner with Bottle-Cycler, finalist in the Premier's Sustainability Awards, as our glass recycling technology partner.

The Stats

In the 12 months we've partnered with Bottle-Cycler, we've saved 4.185 tonnes of CO2, 6,750kg of glass removed and recycled, the equivalent of 1.0051 cars removed from the road, 46.2375 gigajoules of electricity, 16,470 litres of water, and 16.2 m³ of landfill space.

The Importance of Glass Management

Glass recycling is backed by scientific innovation, from crushing to repurposing, with every step focused on reducing waste and increasing sustainability. Bottle-Cycler's on-site glass crushing machine, which lives with us in our marquee space, has allowed us to reduce waste volume by up to 80%, and store up to five times the amount of bottles in the same space as our previous standard recycling practices.

The Recycling Process

Glass recycling begins at the point of collection — contaminated glass can't be recycled, which is why efficient onsite sorting matters. Each bin in our crusher holds up to 300 glass bottles. Once crushed and collected, the glass (known as cullet) is sorted by colour and composition, cleaned of impurities, then melted at a lower temperature than raw materials, saving energy and reducing carbon emissions. From there, companies like Orora remould the recycled glass into new bottles and products — glass can be recycled repeatedly without losing purity or quality.

The Environmental Impact

Recycling glass reduces energy consumption through lower melting points, lowers carbon emissions, conserves natural resources, and contributes to an ongoing, circular economy.

Watch Our Bottle-Cycler in Action

Keen visitors may have noticed our bottle crusher (aka Reginald) at work over a weekend. Next time you're in, ask staff for a demonstration.

title: Five Silver Medals from The Real Review
attribution: Scores and tasting notes from The Real Review
body_html:
We're thrilled to celebrate receiving five Silver Medals for our latest vintage wines from The Real Review.

2021 First Duke Reserve Shiraz — reviewed by Aaron Brasher: "Youthful in the glass. Pretty and fragrant aromas of blueberry, bramble, spice, sarsaparilla, cola and cherry. Medium-bodied, layered and mouth-filling." 91/100, Silver Medal. Drink 2024–2034.

2022 Méthode Traditionnelle Sparkling — reviewed by Huon Hooke: "Pale colour with a good mousse of very fine bubbles and a fresh youthful aroma of almond icing and iced pastries." 92/100, Silver Medal.

2023 Black Spur Gewürztraminer — reviewed by Stuart Knox: "Fragrant lift of lychee and rose petals as expected of this varietal... good length and finishes with freshness that will keep you returning for more." 91/100, Silver Medal. Drink 2024–2028.

2023 Black Spur Chardonnay — reviewed by Aaron Brasher: "Aromas of nuts, grapefruit, green melon and a nettly, green lift... a gentle creaminess to the palate and the finish has real persistence." 91/100, Silver Medal. Drink 2024–2029.

2022 Black Cameron Shiraz — reviewed by Huon Hooke: "Sweet-fruit middle palate then a big and slightly firm finish and aftertaste. Very attractive shiraz." 92/100, Silver Medal. Drink 2024–2034.

title: What's the Ideal Temperature to Drink Wine?
body_html:
When it comes to wine, enjoying it at the ideal temperature is crucial for enhancing its flavours and aromas. Too warm and you'll lose the energy and vibrancy; too cold and you'll dampen its aromatic charm and complexity. Serving temperature is also partly a matter of personal preference, so use this as a guide.

Sparkling: 3–6ºC

Non-vintage Champagne, Australian sparkling and prosecco should be served ice cold. Vintage or aged sparkling is best served a little warmer, closer to a lighter white. Chill for at least two hours before serving.

Lighter Whites: 7–10ºC

Light and bright whites like Grüner Veltliner, Pinot Grigio, Sauvignon Blanc and Gewürztraminer are best enjoyed cold to lock in fresh fruit flavours. Chill for a little over an hour.

Rosé: 7–10ºC

Refreshment is essential for rosé. For dry styles, temperatures closer to lighter whites will unlock fruity notes and round out the acidity.

Richer Whites: 10–14ºC

Oak-influenced Chardonnay loses its complexity if served too cold. Skip the ice bucket — let the bottle sit and unfurl on the table. Take it out 30 minutes before serving.

Lighter Reds: 12–15ºC

Pinot Noir, cool climate Grenache and Sangiovese are best served slightly cool to preserve their crisp acidity and fruit-forward character. Chill for around 15 minutes before serving if needed.

Deeper Reds: 15–18ºC

Australian room temperature typically sits above 20ºC, too warm for full-bodied reds like our Black Cameron Shiraz, Cabernet Sauvignon and First Duke Reserve Shiraz. A short stint in the fridge restores freshness, balance and finesse.

title: Rally to Support Cystic Fibrosis
body_html:
In early September, Boat O General Manager Travers Graham, his wife Alison, and his father and founder of Boat O'Craigo, Steve, completed the annual 'Great Escape' car rally through outback NSW.

This rally supports vital research and development into a cure for Cystic Fibrosis, a cause we at Boat O'Craigo are very passionate about and have supported for many years. Our team of 12, called 'FTroop', had an action-packed week raising awareness for Cystic Fibrosis amongst the community, whilst contributing more than $30,000 in funds raised to this very important cause.

title: Celebrating Beaujolais Day: A Toast to Gamay in the Yarra Valley
body_html:
Every November, wine enthusiasts around the world look forward to International Beaujolais Day — a celebration of Beaujolais Nouveau, the first wines of the new harvest, made exclusively from the Gamay grape. Here in the Yarra Valley, we embrace the day by highlighting the unique expressions of Gamay grown on our local terroir.

The Allure of the Gamay Grape

Gamay is known for its light body and naturally high acidity, producing wines that are bright, fresh and fruit-forward: red berry fruits (cherry, strawberry, raspberry), floral aromas of violet or peony, a subtle earthiness, and light, silky tannins.

Gamay in the Yarra Valley

While most associated with France's Beaujolais region, Gamay has found a welcoming home in our cool climate, where slow ripening enhances bright acidity and complex flavour. Yarra Valley Gamay often shows enhanced freshness, balanced acidity, and layered fruit notes like plum or cranberry alongside classic cherry and raspberry.

Taste Our Gamay

Boat O'Craigo proudly released our first vintage of 2023 N'oubliez Gamay earlier this year. Read the tasting notes and shop it here.

title: Introducing Our 2023 Méthode Traditionnelle Sparkling
body_html:
We are thrilled to announce the release of our 2023 Méthode Traditionnelle Sparkling Wine, a testament to our dedication to excellence and craftsmanship.

The Craftsmanship Behind the Bubbles

Crafted using the traditional method — the same process used for the finest Champagnes — this single-vineyard sparkling was handcrafted from selected clones of Chardonnay (80%) and Pinot Noir (20%) grown on our cool-climate Healesville vineyard, with secondary fermentation in the bottle developing its fine bubbles and complex flavours.

Tasting Notes

A luminous, pale gold hue with an effervescent stream of delicate bubbles. An alluring bouquet of brioche, fresh dough and citrus blossom gives way to white peach and fresh nectarine on the palate, amplified by an extended period on lees.

Perfect Pairings

Oysters and shellfish, soft cheeses like Brie or Camembert, sushi and sashimi, roast poultry, and fruit-forward light desserts like lemon tart or panna cotta.

Visit our Cellar Door to experience this new release, or shop online.

title: N'oubliez Gamay Review
attribution: Score and tasting note from Stuart Knox, The Real Review
body_html:
We're thrilled to share that our 2023 N'oubliez Gamay has been awarded 91 points and 4 Stars by Stuart Knox from The Real Review.

"Medium deep ruby into a purple tinged rim. Raspberry and musk aromas. Light and airy, juicy raspberry fruits with musk and violets lifting along the line. Good length on the back of fine tannins and a fine mesh of acidity. Drink 2025–2029." — Stuart Knox

Shop the 2023 N'oubliez Gamay here.

title: Vintage 2025 Wrap Up
body_html:
People ask, "How was vintage?" We reply, "Fruitful." Some laugh, and some look confused — but fruitful is a thing. With mild, warm weather during flowering and fruit set, the growing season started ideally and continued so: no extreme extended heat, cool nights, and perfectly timed top-up rains gave the vines exactly what they needed to thrive.

With happy vines came more berries and slightly bigger bunches. Early varieties ripened a little earlier than usual (Gewürztraminer and Pinot Grigio), then the weather moderated further to provide consistent, predictable development — leading to lovely, delicious flavours, optimal ripeness and balanced acids across all varieties. Fruitful equals excellent.

title: Savour the Season: The Best Wines to Sip This Autumn
body_html:
As the leaves turn gold and the air takes on that crisp, nostalgic edge, autumn in the Yarra Valley becomes something of a love letter to wine lovers. Here are the wines you should be reaching for this season.

1. Pinot Noir — The Soul of the Valley

Silky, lifted and earthy — look for cherry, wild strawberry and subtle spice. Try our Black Spur Pinot Noir, elegant and expressive with vibrant red fruit and spicy oak.

2. Chardonnay — Cool Climate Sophistication

Modern Yarra Valley Chardonnay is refined and textural. Try our Black Spur Chardonnay, layered with white peach, cashew and flinty minerality.

3. Grüner Veltliner — The Aromatic Wildcard

Vibrant citrus, gentle spice, food-friendly acidity. Try our Black Spur Grüner Veltliner, fresh and textural with green apple aromatics.

4. Rosé — Don't Put It Away Just Yet

More structured, savoury autumn rosé pairs effortlessly with charcuterie, salmon or Friday takeaway. Try our Black Spur Rosé, delicately dry with strawberry, blood orange and citrus peel.

5. Shiraz — Cool Climate, But Make It Elegant

Spice, lifted florals and peppery energy without the jamminess of warmer regions. Try our Black Cameron Shiraz, medium-bodied with dark cherry, blood plum and silky tannins.

A Note on Visiting the Valley

Autumn is an exceptional time to visit — changing colours, cosy cellar doors and seasonal produce at its peak. Make a day of it, or stay a little longer.

title: Perfect Pairings: Discovering the Magic of Cool Climate Shiraz
body_html:
There's something truly special about Shiraz grown in cool-climate regions — elegance, balance, and complexity all wrapped into one delicious pour. Our Black Cameron Shiraz is a proud example of just how refined and expressive Yarra Valley Shiraz can be.

Named in tribute to the Scottish heritage of the Graham family, this wine is crafted from fruit grown on our Kangaroo Ground vineyard, where cooler temperatures and long ripening periods result in a more restrained, perfumed style of Shiraz.

Tasting Notes: Black Cameron Shiraz

A medium-bodied wine with lifted aromas of dark plum, black cherry and white pepper, supported by subtle spice and well-integrated French oak. The palate is beautifully structured with fine tannins and vibrant acidity.

Top Food Pairings

Lamb with Rosemary and Garlic — the wine's peppery spice and dark fruit complement slow-roasted or grilled lamb beautifully.

Mushroom Risotto or Chargrilled Portobello — the umami depth of mushrooms mirrors the wine's spice and subtle oak.

Duck Breast with Blackberry Jus — rich, gamey duck pairs wonderfully with the wine's fruit-forward notes.

Aged Cheddar or Hard Sheep's Cheese — a simple pairing where the wine's acidity and spice cut through the richness.

Serving Suggestions

Decant for 20–30 minutes before serving, use a large red wine glass, and serve slightly cooler than room temperature, around 16–18°C.

The Black Cameron Shiraz is available for tasting at our cellar door and online.

title: Boat O'Craigo Selected in Top 10 Yarra Valley Small Wineries
attribution: List excerpt from Visit Victoria's Top 10 Yarra Valley Small Wineries
body_html:
We're proud to have been named among Visit Victoria's Top 10 Yarra Valley Small Wineries, alongside Seville Hill, Payne's Rise, Seville Estate, Soumah of Yarra Valley, Jayden Ong Winery & Cellar Bar, Steels Gate Wines, Yering Farm Wines, Tokar Estate Winery and Mandala.

Of Boat O'Craigo, Visit Victoria writes: "Boutique family-owned Boat O'Craigo has a reputation for outstanding hand-crafted wines. Graze on antipasto, cheese paddles and traditional thin-base pizza with a glass of wine from the cellar door while taking in the majestic views."

Read the full list on the Visit Victoria website.

title: Boat O'Craigo Partners with the Australian Master of the Amateurs Championship
body_html:
We are proud to announce that Boat O'Craigo will be an official sponsor of the Australian Master of the Amateurs Championship from 2026, committing to a three-year partnership that celebrates excellence, tradition, and community.

Held annually at the prestigious Southern Golf Club in Melbourne, the Australian Master of the Amateurs is recognised as the number one amateur golf championship in Australia and one of the most elite amateur tournaments in the world, bringing together the globe's top-ranked amateur golfers for four days of world-class competition.

As part of the partnership, Boat O'Craigo will pour a selection of our premium Yarra Valley wines, offering tastings and onsite sales during the tournament, with our team onsite to connect with players, spectators and fellow sponsors. We're especially delighted to work closely with Directors Angela and Peter Mann, whose passion and professionalism have made the tournament a standout on the international golf calendar.

We look forward to raising a glass at the 2026 championship and beyond. Learn more at the tournament website.

title: Farewelling Our Vineyard Manager and Friend, Andrew Mills
body_html:
Our leadership team recently had a lovely lunch in the city to bid farewell and best wishes to our long-term Vineyard Manager, Andrew Mills. Those who have joined our annual Pick & Stomp event in March will remember him fondly.

Andrew lovingly managed both our Healesville and Kangaroo Ground vineyards for 14 years — early mornings, freezing cold, searing heat, snakes, kangaroos, deer, he's seen it all. It all starts with the fruit, and without exception Andrew produced exceptional fruit year on year despite the challenges of weather and environment. His fruit has been the building block of our consistent premium wine.

Over the journey, Andrew became far more a friend than a colleague, always willing to help where it was needed. As Andrew moves into his next chapter, we're in highly capable hands with his protégé and our new Vineyard Manager, Ben Craig, who has been working in Yarra Valley vineyards for 15 years and closely with Andrew for the last two.

Andrew — on behalf of myself, Steve and your Boat O family, a heartfelt thank you for your friendship and everything you have done over the last 14 years. We wish you all the very best for your next chapter.

— Travers Graham

title: Boat O'Craigo to Host the James Halliday Chardonnay & Cabernet Challenge
body_html:
Boat O'Craigo is proud to be the host venue for one of Australia's most prestigious wine competitions, the James Halliday Australian Chardonnay & Cabernet Challenge, taking place in the Yarra Valley on 23 and 24 September 2025.

This annual celebration brings together Chardonnay and Cabernet Sauvignon to showcase the very best expressions from across Australia's iconic wine regions, alternating between the Yarra Valley and Margaret River each year. Born in the Yarra Valley in 2012, the Chardonnay Challenge has long been a benchmark for excellence, while the Cabernet Challenge, launched in 2016, has become one of Australia's great success stories; the two were brought together in 2021.

2025 Judging Panel

Chair of Judges: Glenn Barry (Adelaide Hills, SA). Panel Chairs: Julian Grounds (Mornington, VIC), Lisa Jenkins (Gippsland, VIC), Sarah Pidgeon (Coonawarra, SA). Judges: Alex Beckett (Sunbury, VIC), Matt Buchan (Margaret River, WA), Matt Turnbull (Adelaide Hills, SA), Xanthe Hatcher (Hunter Valley, NSW), Penny Jones (Tasmania), Greg Clack (Adelaide Hills, SA).

The Challenge is a collaborative initiative between the Margaret River Wine Association and Wine Yarra Valley. As James Halliday noted when the Challenges were first combined: "The decision to bring the Chardonnay and Cabernet Sauvignon Challenges together provides a win-win scenario for all stakeholders. The results will provide a unique snapshot of the state of play of these two great varieties."

For Boat O'Craigo, hosting this event is both an honour and a celebration of our role within the Yarra Valley community. We look forward to welcoming the judges, participants and guests to our winery in September.

title: Celebrate International Champagne Day with Méthode Traditionnelle Sparkling
body_html:
On International Champagne Day, wine enthusiasts around the world gather to celebrate the sparkling wines that bring joy to countless occasions — and an opportunity to explore sparkling wines crafted using the traditional method, or méthode traditionnelle, a technique that has found remarkable expression in Australia's cool-climate regions.

What is Méthode Traditionnelle?

The same process used in the production of Champagne: a secondary fermentation in the bottle that creates fine bubbles and complex flavours, requiring meticulous care and extended lees ageing to develop depth and texture.

Boat O'Craigo's 2023 Méthode Traditionnelle Sparkling

Crafted from select parcels of Chardonnay and Pinot Noir, this sparkling wine undergoes extended lees ageing, giving it a rich mouthfeel and a fine, persistent mousse, with complex aromas and crisp acidity.

This Champagne Day, join us at Boat O'Craigo to experience our 2023 Méthode Traditionnelle Sparkling — learn more here.

title: Volcanic Roots: How Kangaroo Ground Shapes the Character of Our Wines
body_html:
There's something undeniably captivating about wines grown in volcanic soils. Across the world, regions like Etna in Sicily and Santorini in Greece are celebrated for producing wines of remarkable depth, energy and tension, shaped by the ancient power of fire and earth.

Here in the Yarra Valley, our Kangaroo Ground vineyard shares that same story of origin. Sitting on the valley's western edge, this warmer site is home to rich black volcanic basalt soils, forged long ago by eruptions that once defined the landscape. Combined with north-east facing slopes that capture the morning sun, it's the perfect environment for wines of exceptional structure, intensity and character.

Volcanic soils are known for their minerality and vitality — free-draining yet nutrient-rich, forcing vines to dig deep for sustenance, resulting in smaller berries with concentrated flavour. At Kangaroo Ground, this translates into our Black Cameron Shiraz, Braveheart Cabernet Sauvignon, and Kincardine Grenache: the Shiraz shows dark plum and spice with a silky texture, the Cabernet Sauvignon balances richness with elegant tannins, and the Grenache offers vibrant red fruit and a savoury finish, all unified by the depth and warmth that only basalt soils can bring.

Kangaroo Ground's volcanic heritage gives our wines a story that stretches back millions of years — a reminder that great wine begins in the earth itself.

title: Why Chardonnay Has Reclaimed Its Throne
body_html:
Chardonnay is having a renaissance, and never has it been more thrilling to drink. From bold, buttery styles of the past to the crisp, mineral-driven expressions of today, the pendulum has swung from the tropical, oak-laden "sunshine in a bottle" wines of the 1980s and 90s, through the "anything but chardonnay" backlash, to today's renewed desire for wines that balance flavour, texture and place.

Meet Our New 2024 Black Spur Chardonnay

Luminous medium straw in the glass; the nose offers lemon curd, stone fruit, almond, honeysuckle and a gentle whisper of buttery popcorn. On the palate it's lively and tactile, with citrus and mineral energy balanced by delicate oak and almond undertones, leading into a long, refined finish.

Sourced from four distinct clones at our cool Healesville site, the fruit was whole-bunch pressed, cold-settled and racked into French oak hogsheads and puncheons (15% new oak), with spontaneous fermentation and partial malolactic fermentation in half the wine, before resting 10 months on lees to build texture and depth.

Chardonnay's strength is its diversity — it can lean crisp and saline or full and textural depending on site and style, rewards nuance, and is as versatile at the table as it is standing alone. Winemakers across Australia are rediscovering its potential, from the traditional cool-climate strongholds of the Yarra Valley and Margaret River to emerging regions in Tasmania, Gippsland and Macedon.

Our 2024 Black Spur Chardonnay is our contribution to that conversation. Visit our cellar door in Healesville to taste this new vintage.

title: Everything You Need to Know About Rosé
body_html:
There's something undeniably joyful about a glass of rosé — the luminous blush colour, and the way it effortlessly bridges the gap between red and white. At Boat O'Craigo we've long believed rosé is more than a summer fling, and our 2024 Black Spur Rosé is proof that pink can be both playful and sophisticated.

What is Rosé, Really?

Rosé is crafted from red grape varieties, given only a brief embrace between the juice and grape skins to capture colour and a whisper of flavour, before being pressed off to ferment like a white. The result is fresh, bright and aromatic, without the weight or tannin of a red. Ours sits firmly in the crisp, dry and elegant camp.

What Does Our 2024 Black Spur Rosé Taste Like?

Wild strawberry, rose petal and pink grapefruit, layered with a gentle creaminess from time spent resting on lees. The palate is all about balance — lively acidity, a touch of savoury texture, and a long, dry finish.

When to Drink It

Pair it with charcuterie, fresh seafood, spicy Asian dishes or a classic niçoise salad — equally at home at a long lunch under the vines or by the fire with a grazing board.

Come taste it at our Healesville cellar door, or shop the 2024 Black Spur Rosé online.

title: Leftover Wine? Here Are Some Smart (and Delicious) Ways to Use It
body_html:
Leftover wine might sound like a myth, but it happens. Once opened, wine begins to change fairly quickly as oxygen dulls flavours, so before you pour it down the sink, here's how to give it a second life.

1. Keep It Fresh for Longer

Preservation systems using an inert gas like argon create a protective layer over the wine to help preserve aroma and flavour. Or simply freeze leftover wine in ice cube trays — not for drinking, but perfect for cooking later.

2. Turn It Into Something New to Drink

Red wine and cola (kalimotxo) — a Basque classic, refreshing and easy. Sangria — forgiving and endlessly adaptable, ideal for slightly tired light-bodied reds. Mulled wine — gently heated with spices, citrus and a little sweetness. New York Sour — leftover red wine layered on top of a whiskey sour for colour, fruitiness and complexity.

3. Cook With It

Braising — red or white adds body and richness to slow-cooked dishes. Deglazing — wine lifts the caramelised flavour stuck to the pan after searing meat. Marinades — even faded wine's acidity helps tenderise and add subtle flavour. Homemade vinegar — with leftover wine, raw vinegar containing the "mother", and patience, you can make your own.

Leftover wine doesn't have to feel like a problem to solve — sometimes the second act is just as good as the first.

title: Discover the Yarra Valley Town Made for Slow Weekends
attribution: Article by Kellie Floyd, originally published in Australian Traveller
body_html:
Australian Traveller journalist Kellie Floyd wrote a feature on slow weekends in Healesville, the Yarra Valley town Boat O'Craigo calls home.

Once a service centre for the goldfields, Healesville has grown into a destination with a main street of cosy cafes, a bookshop, silversmiths, boutiques and an artisan providore, framed by parklands, bush trails, a vintage tourist railway, distilleries and award-winning vineyards. The piece recommends staying at Healesvillas for mountain views, dining at My Little Kitchen Cafe, Herd or No. 7 Healesville, and drinking at Four Pillars Gin, Boat O'Craigo and Zoncello Yarra Valley. Other highlights include Silvermist Studio, Morris et al, Verso Books, Healesville Sanctuary, Maroondah Reservoir Park and the Yarra Valley Railway.

Read the full original article at Australian Traveller.
